title: Grandma texts back
summary: A grandmother in Toronto and her grandson text in two half-languages, and slowly meet in the middle.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Hi Grandma! How are you? I am at school. We have a test today. I am nervous.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Leo. You write so fast. I am slow. My fingers are big and the letters are small. I am good. The soup is on the stove.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Haha. What is the soup? Do not let it burn!

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: It is the soup from the old country. Your mother does not like it. You used to eat three bowls when you were small. Do you remember?

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: I remember! With the little white things in it. I do not know the English word.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Dumplings. In my language we say a different word. You knew that word before you knew English. Now you forget it. This is normal. But it makes me a little sad.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Teach me the word again. I will put it in my phone. I will not forget this time.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: All right. But you must say it out loud, not only in the phone. A word in a phone is sleeping. A word in your mouth is alive.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: OK. After my test, I will call you and say it. And you will tell me if it is right.

body.speaker: Grandma
body.text: Yes. And bring your test home. We will eat the soup together. You can be nervous here, with a hot bowl. That is better than being nervous alone at school.

body.speaker: Leo
body.text: Deal. Save me three bowls, Grandma. Like before.

annotations.meaningVi: bạn khỏe không / dạo này thế nào

annotations.phrase: A word in a phone is sleeping
annotations.meaningVi: một từ trong điện thoại đang ngủ (ẩn dụ)

grammarNotes.title: 'Used to' for past habits no longer true
grammarNotes.bodyVi: A2 dùng 'used to + V (nguyên thể)' để nói thói quen trong quá khứ nay không còn. Trong bài: 'You used to eat three bowls when you were small.' Nghĩa: hồi nhỏ cháu thường ăn ba bát, GIỜ không còn vậy. Lưu ý: trong câu hỏi và phủ định dùng 'use to' (bỏ 'd'): 'Did you use to eat...?'
grammarNotes.bodyEn: A2 uses 'used to + base verb' for a past habit that is no longer true. From the dialogue: 'You used to eat three bowls when you were small.' Meaning: as a child he regularly ate three bowls, but NOT NOW. Note: in questions and negatives use 'use to' (drop the 'd'): 'Did you use to eat...?'

translationVi:
Leo: Chào Bà! Bà khỏe không? Cháu đang ở trường. Hôm nay tụi cháu có bài kiểm tra. Cháu hồi hộp.

Bà: Leo. Cháu viết nhanh quá. Bà chậm lắm. Ngón tay bà to mà chữ thì nhỏ. Bà khỏe. Nồi súp đang ở trên bếp.

Leo: Haha. Súp gì vậy bà? Đừng để cháy nha!

Bà: Là món súp của quê cũ. Mẹ cháu không thích nó. Hồi nhỏ cháu thường ăn ba bát đấy. Cháu còn nhớ không?

Leo: Cháu nhớ! Có mấy thứ nhỏ màu trắng trong đó. Cháu không biết từ tiếng Anh.

Bà: Dumplings (bánh bao). Trong tiếng của bà mình gọi bằng một từ khác. Cháu biết từ đó trước cả khi biết tiếng Anh. Giờ cháu quên rồi. Điều này là bình thường. Nhưng nó khiến bà hơi buồn.

Leo: Bà dạy cháu lại từ đó đi. Cháu sẽ lưu vào điện thoại. Lần này cháu sẽ không quên.

Bà: Được. Nhưng cháu phải nói nó thành tiếng, đừng chỉ lưu trong điện thoại. Một từ trong điện thoại là đang ngủ. Một từ trong miệng cháu là sống động.

Leo: Dạ. Sau bài kiểm tra, cháu sẽ gọi cho bà và nói từ đó. Và bà sẽ cho cháu biết đúng hay không.

Bà: Ừ. Và mang bài kiểm tra về nhà. Bà cháu mình sẽ cùng ăn súp. Cháu có thể hồi hộp ở đây, với một bát súp nóng. Như vậy còn hơn hồi hộp một mình ở trường.

Leo: Chốt nha. Để dành cho cháu ba bát, Bà ơi. Như ngày xưa.

questions.explanation: 'We have a test today. I am nervous.'

options: Her fingers are big and the letters are small.
questions.explanation: 'My fingers are big and the letters are small.'

options: The soup from the old country.
A new recipe from a book.
questions.explanation: 'It is the soup from the old country.'

questions.prompt: How much soup did Leo eat when he was small?
questions.explanation: 'You used to eat three bowls when you were small.'

questions.prompt: Why is Grandma a little sad?
options: Leo has forgotten the word he knew before English.
questions.explanation: 'You knew that word before... Now you forget it... it makes me a little sad.'

questions.prompt: What does Grandma say about a word in a phone?
options: A word in a phone is sleeping; a word in your mouth is alive.
A word in a phone is safest.
questions.explanation: 'A word in a phone is sleeping. A word in your mouth is alive.'

questions.prompt: What will Leo do after his test?
options: Call Grandma and say the word.
questions.explanation: 'I will call you and say it.'

questions.prompt: What does Grandma offer at the end?
options: To eat the soup together so Leo can be nervous at home, not alone at school.
To do the test for him.
To buy him a new phone.
To move back to the old country.
questions.explanation: 'We will eat the soup together... better than being nervous alone.'

questions.prompt: What does the conversation mainly show about Leo and Grandma's languages?
options: Each speaks part of the other's language, and they meet in the middle through the soup and the word.
They cannot understand each other at all.
Leo refuses to learn her language.
questions.explanation: He texts fast English; she half-English; the lost word bridges them.

options: A grandmother and grandson keep a fading shared language alive through a remembered food and one small word.
questions.explanation: The dumpling word and the saved bowls carry the connection.

Three bowls when you were small.

It makes me a little sad.

A word in your mouth is alive.
I will call you and say it.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Grandma says a word in a phone is 'sleeping' but a word in your mouth is 'alive'. Is there a word, song, or saying from your family or first language that you are slowly forgetting? What is one thing you could do this week to keep it alive?
